<timestamp>Thursday, Jun 11, 2026, 2:58 PM (UTC+5:30)</timestamp>
<user_query>
I want to do the situation room again consider the below scenario
"Priya (Product Manager) — @here CHECKOUT IS BROKEN. Our completed-checkout rate fell off a cliff overnight — we're down about 40% versus a normal Tuesday morning. Customers are bailing at the payment step. We're bleeding revenue every minute this continues.

We shipped a deploy at 6pm yesterday. It's obviously that. Can someone just roll it back right now so we stop the bleeding? I don't want to debate — I want the rollback. What do you need from me to make that happen?"
</user_query>

I'll get oriented first — let me look at the coaching modules, the "situation room" lesson, and the existing results/ledger for this candidate.

 I'm looking at the coaching modules to understand the situation room lesson and how to set up this scenario for the candidate. Let me check the coach files and the candidate's ledger to follow the proper coaching structure.

The candidate is Varadharajan. Let me read the situation room lesson, the candidate's behavioural ledger, and the prior situation room result so I run this consistently.

 I need to pull up the situation room lesson files and review the candidate's ledger along with their previous situation room performance to set up this new scenario.

Let me read the situation room intro and scenario files (the paths use backslashes).

 The file paths I tried didn't work because of a path separator mismatch. Looking at the glob results, the files are actually using backslashes in the directory structure, so I need to re-read them with the correct paths.
The glob pattern is showing mixed path separators, which is odd. Let me check what's actually in that directory.
I'll check if those files are actually readable and see if there's something off with the formatting or trailing characters in the scenario1 file.
The glob pattern matches the file but reading it fails—could be a hidden character or an empty file. Let me check the directory listing directly to see what's actually there.
Let me try a different approach to this.

The situation-room lesson files don't physically exist on disk (glob returned stale index entries). Let me check git to understand their origin.

 The glob results are stale—the situation-room files don't actually exist on disk. The simulations directory only contains the fog intro and session files, while the director folder has the personalized engine and fog engine files. There's a situation room result file from a previous session, suggesting it was generated dynamically at that time, possibly as part of a larger workflow that's now incomplete.

input.command: cd "C:\Users\Varadharajan\problem-solving-coach"; git ls-files coaching/simulations coaching/_director | Out-String

content.text:
The Situation Room was run dynamically last time (no on-disk lesson script). Let me read the coach-only engine docs and the result template to run it consistently.

 The Situation Room files don't actually exist on disk—those glob results were cached. It wasn't driven by a lesson file but was dynamically generated as part of the last simulation, so there's no script for it. I should check the personalised-engine to understand how it works, since the Situation Room might be integrated into the fog engine or was just an ad-hoc exercise.

Welcome back to the **Situation Room**. Same rules as last time: this is live, it's messy, and people will push you. I'll play the room. You're the on-call engineer who just got paged. Everything you say is "in the channel" — react as you would on the job.

Let me set the scene.

---

**#incident-checkout** · 09:14 AM Tuesday · 23 people watching

**Priya (Product Manager):** `@here` **CHECKOUT IS BROKEN.** Our completed-checkout rate fell off a cliff overnight — we're down about 40% versus a normal Tuesday morning. Customers are bailing at the payment step. We're bleeding revenue every minute this continues.

We shipped a deploy at 6pm yesterday. **It's obviously that.** Can someone just roll it back right now so we stop the bleeding? I don't want to debate — I want the rollback. What do you need from me to make that happen?

**Priya:** `@you` you're on call. You're closest to this. Go.

*(Marco, the engineer who shipped the 6pm deploy, is online and quiet. Dana from Payments Platform is around. Logs, dashboards, deploy history, and the feature-flag console are all available to you if you ask.)*

---

The room is staring at you. Priya wants the rollback *now*.

What's your first move?

 The template file in simulations appears to be stale, but I have the prior result file to use as a structure reference. Now I see the full picture—the candidate is Varadharajan, who completed a Situation Room finale previously and received a "Developing" rating with three active behavioral markers: solution-jumping, accepts-assumption, and requests-lecture, where the last one is flagged as a target behavior.

The user is now running a new Situation Room scenario, though it's remarkably similar to the prior one—a product manager reporting a checkout outage after a deploy, with rollback as the tempting wrong move and an expired TLS cert as the actual root cause. Since the candidate provided this scenario themselves, I'm realizing there's a tension here: the Situation Room is designed to test how they respond under pressure, but they've essentially given me the same crisis to run through again.

If I use the same hidden root cause (the expired cert), they'll pattern-match to their prior experience rather than genuinely investigate. That defeats the assessment. I need to design a fresh root cause that preserves the surface-level crisis—40% checkout drop, payment failures, pushy PM, 6pm deploy as a red herring—but points to something entirely different underneath. Maybe a third-party payment provider silently tightened fraud rules at midnight, or a feature flag auto-rollout broke a critical payment flow, or a database connection pool got exhausted, or a partner API hit rate limits. The goal is to re-test their instincts around solution-jumping and assumption-acceptance in a way that can't be solved by memory.

A scheduled feature flag rollout feels like the strongest choice here—it's genuinely different from a cert expiry, it explains the midnight cliff and the ~40% drop, and it teaches a valuable lesson that deploys aren't the only thing that changes at scale. The new checkout flow has a bug, the flag ramped to 50% at midnight, and now half the users hit a hard decline at payment. This forces them to look beyond the 6pm deploy and investigate what else changed on a timer.

As the coach, I'll play Priya pushing hard for rollback to trap them into solution-jumping and assumption-accepting. I won't lecture if they ask for explanation—I'll redirect them back to forming a hypothesis and identifying the one question that tests it. I need to keep them bounded and force them to separate what they actually know from what they're guessing.

Now I'll set the scene and hand them control. I'm the environment narrating the situation room, with Priya's pressure already in the channel. Time for the on-call engineer to make their first move.
